title: SEGMENT 10 — Engineering antibodies in test tubes — phage display

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — Engineering antibodies in test tubes — phage display
Welcome back.
Before the break, we left off with a problem.
Hybridoma technology was beautiful, but the antibodies it produced were mouse antibodies, and we needed human ones.
There were two routes to solve this.
We'll cover the first route — engineering — in this segment, and the second route — humanization — in the next.
The engineering route turned out to be even more radical than humanization.
The idea was: forget hybridomas.
Forget animals altogether.
What if you could generate antibodies in a test tube, from scratch, completely outside any immune system?
This sounds insane on first hearing.
The immune system generates antibody diversity through V(D)J recombination, B cell development, germinal center selection — a whole orchestrated cellular process.
How would you replicate that in a tube?
The answer is one of the most creative ideas in molecular biology, and it won Greg Winter and George Smith a share of the 2018 Nobel Prize in Chemistry.
The technique is called phage display.
Here is the analogy.
Imagine you have a lock.
You don't know what key opens it.
So you do this: you print every possible key shape — millions of them — onto millions of tiny plastic flags.
Each flag has one key shape stamped on it, and attached to the back of each flag is a tag identifying which key it carries.
You dip all the flags into a vat full of locks.
Most of the flags wash away because their keys don't fit.
But the flags whose keys do fit — those flags stick.
You pull out the stuck flags, read their tags, and now you know which keys worked.
Then you make millions of copies of the winning keys, dip again, refine.

After a few rounds, you've selected, from a starting library of millions of possibilities, the perfect key for your lock — without ever needing a locksmith, without ever needing a mouse, without ever needing biology to do the selecting.
That's phage display in cartoon form.
Now let me make it physical.
A phage — short for bacteriophage — is a small virus that infects bacteria.
It has a protein coat made of a few structural proteins.
The trick of phage display, originally developed by George Smith, was to genetically engineer the phage so that a foreign protein could be fused to one of its coat proteins.
The foreign protein would then be displayed on the outside of the phage particle, sticking out into the world, while the gene encoding that protein would be carried inside the phage.
So the phage particle becomes a physical link between a displayed protein and the gene that encodes it — protein on the outside, blueprint on the inside, both in the same particle.
The flag and its tag.
Now, take that idea and apply it to antibodies.

You build a library — meaning, you take a huge collection of antibody variable-region genes, perhaps millions or even billions of different versions, and you fuse each one to a phage coat protein gene.
You let the phages infect bacteria and produce phage particles.
Each individual phage particle now displays one specific antibody fragment on its surface, with the gene for that antibody fragment packaged inside it.
Your library is millions or billions of phages, each with a different displayed antibody.
To find an antibody against your target, you do something called panning.
You coat a dish with your target molecule.
You pour your phage library over the dish.
Phages whose displayed antibodies don't bind the target wash off when you rinse.
Phages whose displayed antibodies do bind the target stick to the dish.
You collect the bound phages, let them reinfect bacteria to amplify the winners, and then do another round of panning with more stringent conditions.
After a few rounds of select-and-amplify, you've enriched the population for the best binders.
Sequence the genes inside the winning phages, and you have, in your hands, the DNA sequence of an antibody that binds your target.
You can then plug that DNA into a normal cell-based antibody production system and make as much of the antibody as you want.
The power of this technique is enormous.
You can build phage libraries from fully human antibody genes — no mouse involved — so the antibodies you find are human from the start, no humanization needed.
You can build libraries from synthetic sequences, expanding diversity beyond what nature ever produced.

You can pan against targets that are toxic, or self-antigens that an immune system wouldn't normally make antibodies against, because the selection is happening in a dish, not in a body with safety mechanisms.
A whole generation of important drugs has come out of phage display.
Adalimumab — Humira, the anti-TNF drug that until recently was the world's best-selling medication — was discovered by phage display.
It's a fully human antibody against a target involved in autoimmune inflammation, and it was selected from a library of phages, not from an immune system.
The summary you should carry away: phage display is a way to replace biological selection with biochemical selection.
Print every possible key onto tiny lockpicks, then dip them in the lock and see which one turns.
Then read the tag on the lockpick to find out what key shape you have.
It's an industrial-scale, in-vitro version of what the immune system does in your lymph nodes, with the bonus that you can engineer human antibodies from the very start.
